segments.title: Four-funnel liners before Titanic
segments.summary: Titanic and Olympic belonged to a longer international history of four-funnel liners, not a design trend they created.
segments.body: The video starts by putting the famous White Star liners in a broader German, British, and French sequence. German companies had already made the four-funnel form familiar before Cunard's Lusitania and Mauretania, while later ships such as Arundel Castle served routes beyond the North Atlantic. The comparison makes funnels a useful clue to changing commercial routes, national prestige, and marine-engine efficiency rather than a feature unique to Titanic.

evidence.note: The transcript traces four-funnel liners from Kaiser Wilhelm der Grosse through British, French, and postwar examples.

segments.title: Minas Geraes: armour and turret layout
segments.summary: The Minas Geraes class shows how a smaller navy could use unusually comprehensive armour and a practical turret arrangement to deter stronger opponents.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke emphasizes armour carried high on the ship as well as around the belt, gun deck, turrets, and conning positions. He pairs that protection with twelve 12-inch guns, a large secondary battery, wing turrets kept clear of an excessively across-deck firing scheme, and superfiring turrets forward and aft. The design is presented as a serious attempt to turn heavy protection and carefully arranged firepower into a credible deterrent.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the class's layered nine- and twelve-inch armour, armament, wing turrets, and superfiring positions.

segments.title: Olympic-class hybrid propulsion
segments.summary: Titanic and Olympic combined familiar reciprocating engines with a low-pressure turbine, revealing how cautiously even prestige ships adopted new propulsion.
segments.body: The central propeller was driven by a low-pressure turbine while the outboard propellers used four-cylinder reciprocating engines. This mixed arrangement linked mature machinery with a newer technology rather than betting the ship entirely on turbines. The contrast with naval adoption highlights why long-range reliability mattered: a liner had to complete an Atlantic crossing, not merely promise speed on paper.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the central low-pressure turbine, reciprocating outboard engines, and the priority of reliable Atlantic service.

segments.title: Delaware's centerline battery and missed speed
segments.summary: USS Delaware demonstrates the advantages of a centerline main battery—and the speed potential sacrificed by retaining a fifth turret.
segments.body: The Delaware class used superfiring turrets on the centerline, avoiding the compromises associated with wing-turret layouts while retaining an eight-gun broadside comparable with contemporary British ships. Dr. Clarke imagines the alternative: removing a turret to make room for more machinery and a faster, battlecruiser-like ship. Delaware and North Dakota also show the United States experimenting with different propulsion arrangements, from vertical triple-expansion engines to Curtis turbines.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Delaware's superfiring centerline turrets, its eight-gun broadside, propulsion alternatives, and unrealized speed potential.

segments.title: The dreadnought race beyond Germany
segments.summary: Looking beyond Anglo-German rivalry reveals American and Italian technical developments that also shaped Royal Navy attention.
segments.body: The video argues that fleet competition was not only a question of how many German ships existed. In technological terms, the Royal Navy was watching American and Italian developments closely as well. Delaware becomes the case study for a wider point: good hull forms and alternative machinery or armament choices can be obscured when the era is remembered only through famous dreadnoughts and headline rivalries.

evidence.note: The transcript says the Royal Navy watched U.S. and Italian technological advances while treating Germany chiefly as a numerical concern.
